title: Bahrain GP Winners and Losers: Strategy Unpicks a Desert Puzzle, Ferrari Left Without Answers
meta: Published: 13 April 2026 | By Lena Müller, Strategy Analyst | F1CC News
caption: Joshua Nathanial celebrates a breakthrough victory as Bahrain's strategic puzzle reshuffles the championship order.
body:
Let's rewind to lap 14, turn 1. The soft-tyre runners were already losing grip. Philippe Verhaeren had blinked first, diving into the pits, and the leaders were approaching the crossover point where staying out meant losing time and pitting meant trusting the undercut. Quarter Kevu and Jason Roy came in on the same lap, both switching to fresh softs. Roy's stop was clean, but his release dropped him into a knot of cars. Kevu emerged ahead. That single pit-stop phase — the decision to pit both drivers simultaneously — branched the race into two completely different futures. Joshua Nathanial, the medium-tyre starter, stayed out. He covered every phase that followed. And he won the Bahrain Grand Prix.

The strategic pattern of the race was defined by three offset windows. First, an early soft-tyre degradation that forced an early first stop and rewarded the medium starters. Second, a long middle phase where tyre preservation decided who could stretch their stint and who fell into traffic. Third, a late-race crossover where drivers on fresher rubber charged, but only if they had cleared the traffic that the early stoppers had fallen into. Nathanial's McLaren navigated all three. The alternatives that haunted the rest of the grid were stark.

Winners

Joshua Nathanial (McLaren, P1 from P7): Nathanial covered every window. He started on mediums, ran long while the soft-runners burned through their first stint, and emerged in the lead when the field cycled through. His second and third stops were clean, and his pace on the hard compound was metronomic. He crossed the line with a comfortable gap to the Williams pair. What if he had started on softs? He would have been in the same traffic that swallowed Roy, fighting for position instead of controlling the race from the front. The alternative cost would have been a podium at best. He got it right.

Williams — Aygen P2, Tef P3 (started P6 and P17): Both drivers executed a flawless two-stop strategy, but the real victory was the upgrades. Williams brought chassis, sidepods, and suspension — the most comprehensive package of any team — and the car responded. Aygen's race was a study in patience: he managed his tyres through the middle phase, then passed Tef late to secure second. Tef, starting from deep in the pack, gained 14 positions through a combination of smart pit calls and a car that was simply faster than the traffic around him. The what-if that haunts rivals: what if Williams hadn't brought those upgrades? They'd still be fighting for points, not leading the constructors' championship.

Jason Roy (Red Bull, P4 from P2): Roy's race was compromised by that early pit-stop phase — he pitted alongside Kevu and emerged in traffic. But his late charge on fresh soft tyres was the fastest stint of the race. He overtook six cars in the final 15 laps, his pace a reminder of Red Bull's underlying speed. The strategy cost him a podium, but the recovery salvaged valuable points. The alternative? If Red Bull had staggered the stops and given Roy clear air, he could have challenged Nathanial for the win.

Riley Swansson (Ferrari, P9 on debut): Swansson's first race in scarlet was a quiet success. He started 13th, avoided the chaos that consumed his teammate, and used a clean two-stop to creep into the points. Ninth place doesn't make headlines, but it provides a strategic baseline for a driver adapting to new machinery. The alternative — an error or an off-track moment — would have left Ferrari with nothing.

Michiil MaraDöner (Alpine, P5): MaraDöner's race was a masterclass in damage limitation. He started on softs, lost ground in the first pit phase, then fought back through the midfield with a series of overtakes that required precision and patience. His fifth place, after a tense intra-team battle with Kondra, keeps him fifth in the championship. The Alpine's tyre wear is still a vulnerability, but MaraDöner is extracting everything the car has.

Losers

Ferrari (Väisänen P16, no points): The championship leaders brought no upgrades to Bahrain — the only team on the grid to arrive empty-handed. The cost was immediate. Väisänen was eliminated in Q1 for the first time this season and never looked like recovering points. His race became a test session, running in clean air but unable to close the gap to the midfield. The what-if scenario that should terrify Ferrari: what if they had brought even a modest upgrade package? They might have scored. Instead, they leave Bahrain with their constructors' lead gone and their rivals emboldened.

Väisänen said. The numbers agree: their 16-point advantage after Japan has vanished in a single weekend.

Robin Keisner (Audi, P11): Keisner's race was undone not by strategy but by execution. A slow pit stop, the team's second in as many races, cost him track position. Then a collision with Maikel Zwart — for which Keisner, not Zwart, received a penalty — dropped him out of the points.

he said. The alternative: a clean stop and a clean fight with Zwart would have put him in the points. Instead, Audi's momentum from Japan is gone, and they leave Bahrain fourth in the constructors'.

Aston Martin (Springfield P19, Fakt P18): Both cars finished outside the points in a race where tyre management was critical. Springfield called it a reality check. A trackside engineer admitted the upgrades — sidepods, underfloor, suspension — left the car unbalanced, the front end too sharp and the rear too loose. The alternative: a setup change that found the window earlier. Instead, they leave Bahrain scoreless and fall further down the order.

Racing Bulls (Kevu P7, Wright P12): The medium-compound gamble that served Kevu well early on backfired when the tyres fell off a cliff in the final phase. Wright's race was compromised by a pit-stop delay. A senior engineer confessed that something wasn't adding up. The team that dominated 2024 and 2025 now sits sixth. The what-if: if they had switched to softs earlier, could Kevu have held on to a podium? The data will be studied long into the night.

Haas (Konstantaras DNF, Cachinho P17): Konstantaras ran out of fuel on the final lap, a miscalculation that cost Haas a finish. Cachinho, back in the team after the swap, never threatened the points. Team principal Jack Stone acknowledged the frustration. The alternative: accurate fuel management would have at least classified the car. Instead, a DNF and a P17 leave Haas adrift at the bottom.

Bahrain delivered a championship shakeup, and the operating rule for 2026 is now clearer than ever: standing still is falling backwards. Ferrari learned that the hard way. Williams proved that aggressive development pays. Nathanial showed that a driver who covers every phase can win even from the second row. Now, here's the haunting question: what if Ferrari's next upgrades are just a step behind the pack? The lead they built in the opening rounds is already gone. Jeddah, with its high-speed demands and concrete walls, will not wait for them to catch up.
